title: Western Suburbs Magpies

infobox.value: The Fibros,  The Cherry Pickers, Wests,   Western Suburbs,   The Magpies
formed joint venture in 2000 with Balmain Tigers

**8 – [1918](/wiki/NSWRFL_season_1918 "NSWRFL season 1918"), [1925](/wiki/NSWRFL_season_1925 "NSWRFL season 1925"), [1932](/wiki/NSWRFL_season_1932 "NSWRFL season 1932"), [1950](/wiki/NSWRFL_season_1950 "NSWRFL season 1950"), [1958](/wiki/NSWRFL_season_1958 "NSWRFL season 1958"), [1961](/wiki/New_South_Wales_Rugby_League_season_1961#Grand_Final "New South Wales Rugby League season 1961"), [1962](/wiki/New_South_Wales_Rugby_League_season_1962#Grand_Final "New South Wales Rugby League season 1962"), [1963](/wiki/New_South_Wales_Rugby_League_season_1963#Grand_Final "New South Wales Rugby League season 1963")**

**17\* – [1909](/wiki/NSWRFL_season_1909 "NSWRFL season 1909"), [1910](/wiki/NSWRFL_season_1910 "NSWRFL season 1910"), [1912](/wiki/NSWRFL_season_1912 "NSWRFL season 1912"), [1913](/wiki/NSWRFL_season_1913 "NSWRFL season 1913"), [1916](/wiki/NSWRFL_season_1916 "NSWRFL season 1916"), [1933](/wiki/NSWRFL_season_1933 "NSWRFL season 1933"), [1940](/wiki/NSWRFL_season_1940 "NSWRFL season 1940"), [1942](/wiki/NSWRFL_season_1942 "NSWRFL season 1942"), [1953](/wiki/NSWRFL_season_1953 "NSWRFL season 1953"), [1955](/wiki/NSWRFL_season_1955 "NSWRFL season 1955"), [1971](/wiki/NSWRFL_season_1971 "NSWRFL season 1971"), [1983](/wiki/NSWRFL_season_1983 "NSWRFL season 1983"), [1984](/wiki/NSWRL_season_1984 "NSWRL season 1984"), [1987](/wiki/NSWRL_season_1987 "NSWRL season 1987"), [1988](/wiki/NSWRL_season_1988 "NSWRL season 1988"), [1998](/wiki/NRL_season_1998 "NRL season 1998"), [1999](/wiki/NRL_season_1999 "NRL season 1999")**

This section **does not [cite](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources") any [sources](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability")**. Please help [improve this section](/wiki/Special:EditPage/Western_Suburbs_Magpies "Special:EditPage/Western Suburbs Magpies") by [adding citations to reliable sources](/wiki/Help:Refe

(legal name: Western Suburbs District Rugby League Football Club Ltd) is an Australian [rugby league](/wiki/Rugby_league "Rugby league") football club based in the western suburbs of Sydney, New South Wales. Formed in 1908, **Wests**, as the club is commonly referred to, was one of the nine foundation clubs of the first [New South Wales Rugby League](/wiki/New_South_Wales_Rugby_League "New South Wales Rugby League") competition in Australia. The club, as a sole entity, departed the top-flight competition in 1999 after forming a 50–50 joint venture with [Balmain Tigers](/wiki/Balmain_Tigers "Balmain Tigers") to form the [Wests Tigers](/wiki/Wests_Tigers "Wests Tigers"). The club currently fields sides in the NSW State Cup (Canterbury Cup), [Ron Massey Cup](/wiki/Ron_Massey_Cup "Ron Massey Cup") (Opens),[[1]](#cite_note-1) [S.G. Ball Cup](/wiki/S.G._Ball_Cup "S.G. Ball Cup") (Under 19's) and [Harold Matthews Cup](/wiki/Harold_Matthews_Cup "Harold Matthews Cup") (Under 17's) competitions.

The **Western Suburbs Magpies** (legal name: Western Suburbs District Rugby League Football Club Ltd) is an Australian [rugby league](/wiki/Rugby_league "Rugby league") football club based in the western suburbs of Sydney, New South Wales. Formed in 1908, **Wests**, as the club is commonly referred to, was one of the nine foundation clubs of the first [New South Wales Rugby League](/wiki/New_South_Wales_Rugby_League "New South Wales Rugby League") competition in Australia. The club, as a sole entity, departed the top-flight competition in 1999 after forming a 50–50 joint venture with [Balmain Tigers](/wiki/Balmain_Tigers "Balmain Tigers") to form the [Wests Tigers](/wiki/Wests_Tigers "Wests Tigers"). The club currently fields sides in the NSW State Cup (Canterbury Cup), [Ron Massey Cup](/wiki/Ron_Massey_Cup "Ron Massey Cup") (Opens),[[1]](#cite_note-1) [S.G. Ball Cup](/wiki/S.G._Ball_Cup "S.G. Ball Cup") (Under 19's) and [Harold Matthews Cup](/wiki/Harold_Matthews_Cup "Harold Matthews Cup") (Under 17's) competitions.

[Campbelltown Stadium](/wiki/Campbelltown_Stadium "Campbelltown Stadium"), which has a capacity of 18,000, is the club's home stadium.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Western_Suburbs_Magpies&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6e/Performance_Chart_NRL_WES.svg/250px-Performance_Chart_NRL_WES.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Performance_Chart_NRL_WES.svg)

Chart of yearly table positions for Western Suburbs Magpies in First Grade Rugby League

The club was one of the foundation members of the Sydney rugby football league competition in 1908. Founded at a meeting on 4 February 1908 at Ashfield Town Hall, they won only one match the following season and are sometimes considered the League's first [wooden spooners](/wiki/Wooden_spoon_(award) "Wooden spoon (award)") (the distinction is disputed with Cumberland, who are sometimes considered to have been awarded an extra bye due to missing the start of the competition).[[2]](#cite_note-Collis_p120-2) Though they spent long periods of time as also-rans, they did taste premiership success four times in the mid-20th century. They won their first premiership in 1930, beating St George 27–2.[[3]](#cite_note-3) Four years later they defeated Eastern Suburbs to win their second title.[[4]](#cite_note-4) For the [1944 NSWRFL season](/wiki/1944_NSWRFL_season "1944 NSWRFL season"), Queensland 1910s representative player [Henry Bolewski](/wiki/Henry_Bolewski "Henry Bolewski") became coach the Western Suburbs club, replacing [Alf Blair](/wiki/Alf_Blair "Alf Blair"), who moved to [South Sydney](/wiki/South_Sydney_Rabbitohs "South Sydney Rabbitohs").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Wests slightly improved on their performance from the previous season, finishing 5th (out of 8), but failing to make the finals, and Bolewski was replaced by club great, [Frank McMillan](/wiki/Frank_McMillan "Frank McMillan").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Wests won a second pair of premierships, beating Balmain in 1948[[5]](#cite_note-5) and South Sydney 22–12 in 1952.[[6]](#cite_note-6) Both times they defeated a club hunting its third title in a row.

### 1960s

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Western_Suburbs_Magpies&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: 1960s")]

Apart from these occasions, the club was famous for three successive grand final matches in 1961, 1962 and 1963 against the [St George Dragons](/wiki/St_George_Dragons "St George Dragons") in the midst of their 11-premiership run. The club boasted footballers such as halfback [Arthur Summons](/wiki/Arthur_Summons "Arthur Summons"), [Harry 'Bomber' Wells](/wiki/Harry_Wells_(rugby_league) "Harry Wells (rugby league)"), [Kel O'Shea](/wiki/Kel_O%27Shea "Kel O'Shea"), [Noel Kelly](/wiki/Noel_Kelly_(rugby_league) "Noel Kelly (rugby league)") and [Peter Dimond](/wiki/Peter_Dimond "Peter Dimond"). The 1963 grand final was immortalised in a photograph which became known as '[The Gladiators](/wiki/Arthur_Summons#The_Gladiators "Arthur Summons")' after St. George captain [Norm Provan](/wiki/Norm_Provan "Norm Provan") and Summons trudged off the field together.

### 1970s

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Western_Suburbs_Magpies&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: 1970s")]

A final period of glory beckoned in the late 1970s where they spent a few years at the top or near-top of the table, yet failed to make a grand final. Coached by [Roy Masters](/wiki/Roy_Masters_(sport) "Roy Masters (sport)") and boasting such players as fullback [John Dorahy](/wiki/John_Dorahy "John Dorahy"), half [Tommy Raudonikis](/wiki/Tommy_Raudonikis "Tommy Raudonikis"), five-eighth [Graeme O'Grady](/wiki/Graeme_O%27Grady "Graeme O'Grady"), [Gavin Miller](/wiki/Gavin_Miller "Gavin Miller"), [Ron Giteau](/wiki/Ron_Giteau "Ron Giteau"), [Les Boyd](/wiki/Les_Boyd "Les Boyd"), prop [John Donnelly](/wiki/John_Donnelly_(rugby_league) "John Donnelly (rugby league)") and five-eighth [Terry Lamb](/wiki/Terry_Lamb "Terry Lamb"). However attractive offers from other clubs and then doubts about the club's viability led to years of exodus of talent. Wests did manage to win the [1977 Amco Cup](/wiki/1977_Amco_Cup "1977 Amco Cup").[[7]](#cite_note-7)

### 1980s

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Western_Suburbs_Magpies&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: 1980s")]

[John Ribot](/wiki/John_Ribot "John Ribot"), a winger for Wests, was the top try-scorer for the 1980 season. In 1983 the NSWRFL attempted to expel Wests from the competition, but a prolonged legal battle to keep their spot ensued, unlike the [Newtown Jets](/wiki/Newtown_Jets "Newtown Jets") who did not compete that year. [Richard Conti](/wiki/Richard_Conti "Richard Conti") appeared against [New South Wales Rugby League](/wiki/New_South_Wales_Rugby_League "New South Wales Rugby League") bosses [John Quayle](/wiki/John_Quayle_(rugby_league) "John Quayle (rugby league)") and [Colin Love](/wiki/Colin_Love "Colin Love") in 1985, successfully advocating to keep the embattled club in the competition. He was later appointed the chairman of the NSWRL Judiciary.

Eventually, Wests relocated to [Campbelltown](/wiki/Campbelltown,_New_South_Wales "Campbelltown, New South Wales") in 1987. Ironically, this was where Newtown had unsuccessfully tried to move to four years earlier. The move to Campbelltown did not change the clubs fortunes immediately with Western Suburbs finishing last in 1987 and 1988. The 1980s proved to be a tough time for the club with Wests claiming four wooden spoons throughout the decade and finishing second last on two occasions.[[8]](#cite_note-8)

### 1990s

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Western_Suburbs_Magpies&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: 1990s")]

Wests began a rebuilding process in the late 1980s and early 1990s. [Laurie Freier](/wiki/Laurie_Freier "Laurie Freier") started the [1988 Winfield Cup](/wiki/1988_Winfield_Cup "1988 Winfield Cup") season as the club's coach but was replaced during the season by [John Bailey](/wiki/John_Bailey_(rugby_league) "John Bailey (rugby league)").[[9]](#cite_note-9)

The club made the semi-finals in 1991 and 1992 under coach [Warren Ryan](/wiki/Warren_Ryan "Warren Ryan"). Wests were NSWRL Club Champions in 1991 when all three grades made the semi-finals. The team also made it to the pre-season Challenge Cup final in 1993 but was beaten by a star-studded Canberra side.

The club then slipped down the ladder and the coaching [reins](/wiki/Rein#In_popular_expression "Rein") were handed over to caretaker [Wayne Ellis](/wiki/Wayne_Ellis_(rugby_league) "Wayne Ellis (rugby league)"). The decision to appoint Tommy Raudonikis as coach for the start of the [1995 season](/wiki/1995_ARL_season "1995 ARL season") sent a shot of adrenaline into the club.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Raudonikis took the Magpies to the finals in 1996. However the club could not compete in the player market in 1997 thanks to the Super League war which saw players' contracts soar sky high to unsustainable levels.[[10]](#cite_note-10)

In 1998 and 1999, a Magpie team filled with many unknown players struggled to be competitive and twice received the wooden spoon. With the club struggling on-field and trying to compete financially against clubs with News Limited funding, the writing was on the wall.[[11]](#cite_note-11)

The well documented [Super League War](/wiki/Super_League_War "Super League War") in 1997 between Super League (News Ltd) and the Australian Rugby League (ARL) resulted in a compromise that by the year 2000, the National Rugby League (NRL) competition would be contested by only 14 teams. With Western Suburbs struggling on field in the NRL competition, it was decided by the club in the middle of 1999 that to survive the cull they would be required to merge their senior team with another club's team. After initial talks with the Canterbury-Bankstown Bulldogs failed an agreement was reached with the Balmain Tigers on 27 July 1999. The Wests Tigers first competed in the 2000 competition. The club also merged its playing colours from the two joint venture partners.

## Current days

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Western_Suburbs_Magpies&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Current days")]

The Western Suburbs Magpies home matches are played at [Lidcombe Oval](/wiki/Lidcombe_Oval "Lidcombe Oval"), [Lidcombe](/wiki/Lidcombe "Lidcombe") and their training facilities are near by.

In 2000 the Western Suburbs Magpies Under 20s team were runners up to the Canterbury Bankstown Bulldogs, captained by Mitch Zammit and Luke Duffy. The Western Suburbs Magpies last title was won in 2002 when the Western Suburbs Magpies captained by Liam Fulton won the New South Wales Rugby League Under 18's competition.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

From 2000 to 2012, the Western Suburbs Magpies fielded teams in the NSWRL First Grade competition now known as the [NSW Cup](/wiki/NSW_Cup "NSW Cup").

In 2006, the Wests Tigers on advice from [Tim Sheens](/wiki/Tim_Sheens "Tim Sheens") pushed the notion that the NRL side would be better served with a single NSW Cup side.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Western Suburbs board opposed this decision and decided to continue fielding their own team. Wests Tigers then allocated all NRL contracted players to the Balmain Tigers (the Wests Magpies had six junior players in that group, as opposed to one from Balmain), citing the advantages of both the NRL and NSW Cup teams training together at [Concord Oval](/wiki/Concord_Oval "Concord Oval"). This continued from 2006 to 2007.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Magpies chairman Kevin Hammond was disappointed at the Wests Tigers decision and informed the Wests Tigers of that in 2008, if the Magpies did not receive a fair share of player allocation from the [Wests Tigers](/wiki/Wests_Tigers "Wests Tigers"), they would form a feeder partnership with rivals [Canberra Raiders](/wiki/Canberra_Raiders "Canberra Raiders"). The Wests Tigers capitulated.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The Western Suburbs Magpies made the semi-finals in 2008, 2009 and 2010. In 2012 funding was withheld from the Magpies by Wests Ashfield Leagues Club Directors (not unanimously) as they now supported Wests Tigers proposal for a single NSW Cup side, even though this was done simply because Balmain couldn't afford a side and Wests could.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

This also went against the Magpies Football Club members wishes and they organised a Protest Rally through the streets of [Campbelltown](/wiki/Campbelltown,_New_South_Wales "Campbelltown, New South Wales").[[13]](#cite_note-13) Wests Ashfield Leagues Club under pressure reinstated funding late in the off season after the Magpies players had already signed on to play with other teams.

The Wests Tigers however decided to cease all support of the Magpies NSW Cup side while continuing support for [Balmain Ryde Eastwood Tigers](/wiki/Balmain_Ryde_Eastwood_Tigers "Balmain Ryde Eastwood Tigers"). The Magpies failed to win a game in 2012 while Balmain Ryde Eastwood Tigers made it to the Grand Final where they were defeated by the [Newtown Jets](/wiki/Newtown_Jets "Newtown Jets").[[14]](#cite_note-14)

The Magpies dropped back to the third tier [Ron Massey Cup](/wiki/Ron_Massey_Cup "Ron Massey Cup") in 2013,[[15]](#cite_note-15) with members voting to field a separate NSW Cup team in 2014, although this did not eventuate.[[16]](#cite_note-16) In 2015, The Magpies added a team in the [Sydney Shield](/wiki/Sydney_Shield "Sydney Shield") competition to act as a feeder for their Ron Massey Cup campaign. In 2016, Western Suburbs made the preliminary final match against [St Mary's](/wiki/St_Mary%27s_Saints "St Mary's Saints") in The Ron Massey Cup but lost the match in a close tussle.[[17]](#cite_note-17) 2017 proved to be a bad year for Wests as The Ron Massey Cup side finished with the wooden spoon and The Sydney Shield side finished second last.[[18]](#cite_note-18)[[19]](#cite_note-19)

On 5 June 2017, it was announced that Western Suburbs had placed a bid to be included into the [Intrust Super Premiership](/wiki/Intrust_Super_Premiership "Intrust Super Premiership") season for 2018.[[20]](#cite_note-20) The re-entry to the [Intrust Super Premiership](/wiki/Intrust_Super_Premiership "Intrust Super Premiership") ended the clubs five-year hiatus from the top level [NSWRL](/wiki/NSWRL "NSWRL") competition that it had participated continuously in from 1908 to 2012. For its return the team was coached by former Western Suburbs first grade player [Brett Hodgson](/wiki/Brett_Hodgson "Brett Hodgson").[[21]](#cite_note-21) In 2018, Wests enjoyed a solid return to the [Intrust Super Premiership NSW](/wiki/Intrust_Super_Premiership_NSW "Intrust Super Premiership NSW") after finishing in 5th place on the table. In week 1 of the finals, Wests were defeated by the [Wyong Roos](/wiki/Wyong_Roos "Wyong Roos") 18–14 in the elimination final thus ending their season.[[22]](#cite_note-22)

At the end of the [2019 Canterbury Cup NSW](/wiki/2019_Canterbury_Cup_NSW "2019 Canterbury Cup NSW") season, Western Suburbs missed out on the finals after finishing in 10th place. Western Suburbs had a difficult year during the 2022 NSW Cup finishing 11th on the table just one spot above wooden spooners [Blacktown Workers](/wiki/Blacktown_Workers_Sea_Eagles "Blacktown Workers Sea Eagles").[[23]](#cite_note-23)[[24]](#cite_note-24)
Western Suburbs had a tough year in the 2024 NSW Cup finishing last and claiming the wooden spoon. Wests only managed to win six games for the entire campaign. In the 2025 NSW Cup season, Western Suburbs improved on the field finishing 9th on the table.[[25]](#cite_note-25)

## Home grounds

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Western_Suburbs_Magpies&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Home grounds")]

[St Luke's Park](/wiki/St_Luke%27s_Park "St Luke's Park") was the home ground of the Western Suburbs club in [1910](/wiki/1910_NSWRFL_season "1910 NSWRFL season") and [1911](/wiki/1911_NSWRFL_season "1911 NSWRFL season").[[26]](#cite_note-26) They started playing their matches at [Pratten Park](/wiki/Pratten_Park "Pratten Park") in [Ashfield](/wiki/Ashfield,_New_South_Wales "Ashfield, New South Wales") in 1912, but after pressure from local residents there the council refused the club permission to use that ground, forcing them to return to St. Luke's Oval from 1915 to 1919.[[27]](#cite_note-27) The Magpies have played at three home grounds since foundation. They returned to Pratten Park in 1920, remaining there until 1966, the club then played at Pratten Park sporadically over the next two decades playing games there in 1971, 1972, 1973, 1977 and 1985. The final ever first grade game to be played at Pratten Park was on 18 August 1985 against Penrith, Penrith won the match by 42–16.[[28]](#cite_note-28) They then moved to [Lidcombe Oval](/wiki/Lidcombe_Oval "Lidcombe Oval") in 1967.[[29]](#cite_note-29) This was due to Ashfield council not allowing the club to play matches on a Sunday (as this was the church day). In 1987, they moved to [Campbelltown](/wiki/Campbelltown,_New_South_Wales "Campbelltown, New South Wales")'s [Orana Park](/wiki/Orana_Park "Orana Park") (located in adjacent Leumeah), which, after a $25 million renovation, is now called [Campbelltown Stadium](/wiki/Campbelltown_Stadium "Campbelltown Stadium") which is now one of the home grounds of the [Wests Tigers](/wiki/Wests_Tigers "Wests Tigers"). The Magpies' lower grade sides play most home games at Campbelltown Stadium, with the occasional game moved to Lidcombe Oval.

## District Junior competition

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Western_Suburbs_Magpies&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: District Junior competition")]

The Western Suburbs District Junior Rugby League (WSDJRL) was a completely separate entity to the Western Suburbs Magpies DRLFC. They were known as the "Junior Magpies", and administered the junior rugby league on behalf of the Western Suburbs Magpies and the [Wests Tigers](/wiki/Wests_Tigers "Wests Tigers") joint venture. It consisted of a network of affiliated junior rugby league clubs throughout the greater [Campbelltown](/wiki/Campbelltown,_New_South_Wales "Campbelltown, New South Wales") and [Liverpool](/wiki/Liverpool "Liverpool") areas of southwestern Sydney. As of 2016 the following clubs were involved in the WSDJRL;

* All Saints JRLFC
* All Stars Glenquarie (formally Macquarie Fields Hawks)
* [Campbelltown City Kangaroos](/wiki/Campbelltown_City_Kangaroos "Campbelltown City Kangaroos") JRLFC
* Campbelltown Collegians JRLFC
* Campbelltown Warriors JRLFC
* Eaglevale St Andrews JRLFC
* East Campbelltown Eagles JRLFC
* Hinchinbrook Hornets JRLFC
* Ingleburn RSL Tigers JRLFC
* Liverpool Catholic Club Raiders JRLFC
* Macarthur Saints JRLFC (ex-St Thomas More JRLFC)
* [Minto Cobras](/wiki/Minto_Cobras "Minto Cobras") JRLFC
* Valley United Vikings JRLFC (formally known as East Valley United and also Green Valley United)

The league was placed into administration in January 2019, with the NSWRL stepping in to manage the competition.

The following clubs also competed in the WSDJRL, but have now folded:

* Airds Colts JRLFC
* Ashcroft JRLFC
* Claymore Panthers JRLFC
* Dayments Dolphins JRLFC
* Heckenberg JRLFC
* Ingleburn Bulldogs JRLFC
* Leumeah Wolves JRLFC
* Liverpool City JRLFC
* Liverpool Titans JRLFC
* Liverpool RSL JRLFC
* Macquarie Cobras JRLFC
* Warwick Farm JRLFC
* Woodlands JRLFC
* Sadlier Bulldogs JRLFC

The WSDJRL has produced many ex and current [Wests Tigers](/wiki/Wests_Tigers "Wests Tigers") players including [Brett Hodgson](/wiki/Brett_Hodgson "Brett Hodgson") (Eagle Vale-St Andrews & Ingleburn RSL), [Dean Collis](/wiki/Dean_Collis "Dean Collis") (Campbelltown Warriors), [Bryce Gibbs](/wiki/Bryce_Gibbs_(rugby_league) "Bryce Gibbs (rugby league)") (All Saints Liverpool), [Shannon Gallant](/wiki/Shannon_Gallant "Shannon Gallant") (All Saints Liverpool & Campbelltown City) [Chris Lawrence](/wiki/Chris_Lawrence_(rugby_league) "Chris Lawrence (rugby league)") (Eagle Vale), and [Shannon McDonnell](/wiki/Shannon_McDonnell_(rugby_league) "Shannon McDonnell (rugby league)") (All Saints Liverpool). David Noaofaluma (Campbelltown Warriors, Campbelltown Collegians) James Tedesco (Eagle Vale St. Andrews) also Camden Rams (group 6, CRL) Other notable [NRL](/wiki/NRL "NRL") players who grew up playing junior rugby league in the WSDJRL are [Anthony Minichiello](/wiki/Anthony_Minichiello "Anthony Minichiello") (East Valley United), [Mark Minichiello](/wiki/Mark_Minichiello "Mark Minichiello") (Liverpool Catholic Club) [Frank Pritchard](/wiki/Frank_Pritchard "Frank Pritchard") (Campbelltown City)[Eric Grothe, Jr.](/wiki/Eric_Grothe,_Jr. "Eric Grothe, Jr.") (Eagle Vale), [Ryan Hoffman](/wiki/Ryan_Hoffman "Ryan Hoffman") (Campbelltown Collegians), [Ben Roberts](/wiki/Ben_Roberts_(rugby_league) "Ben Roberts (rugby league)") (Narellan Jets), [Sauaso Sue](/wiki/Sauaso_Sue "Sauaso Sue") (Macquarie Field Hawks), [Israel Folau](/wiki/Israel_Folau "Israel Folau"), [Jarryd Hayne](/wiki/Jarryd_Hayne "Jarryd Hayne"), [Krisnan Inu](/wiki/Krisnan_Inu "Krisnan Inu"), [Michael Lett](/wiki/Michael_Lett "Michael Lett") (Ingleburn RSL), [Gray Viane](/wiki/Gray_Viane "Gray Viane"), [John Skandalis](/wiki/John_Skandalis "John Skandalis"), [Ken McGuinness](/wiki/Ken_McGuinness "Ken McGuinness"), [Kevin McGuinness](/wiki/Kevin_McGuinness "Kevin McGuinness"), [Tim Lafai](/wiki/Tim_Lafai "Tim Lafai") and [Mickey](/wiki/Mickey_Paea "Mickey Paea") & [Lopini Paea](/wiki/Lopini_Paea "Lopini Paea") (all Minto Cobras).

The first year of the WSDJRL was 1910 and the final standings were: Parramatta Iona 16, Parramatta District 14, Campsie Triers 11, Granville Royals 11, Enfield Mercantile 9, Ashfield Surryville 7, Auburn Park 2. Since the NSWRL had promised 2 sets of medals the teams then split into 2 grades, Campsie Triers beating Granville Royals in a playoff to go onto the A section and the other 4 teams in the B Finals. Parramatta District won the A Grade and Granville Royals the B Grade. Parramatta District went into the NSWRL 3rd Grade competition in 1911 and Granville Royals joined them in 1912. Campsie Triers and Enfield Mercantile were formed by a split in the Enfield Federals club that had won the B Grade of the WSJRU competition in 1909, in its first year as a club. Val Howell, Frank Howell, S Gagan, G Gagan, A Tanner and Thompson joined Campsie while Lewis, H McCoy, D Nicholls, Prentice and Smythe joined Mercantile. They were together as the Feds in 1911 and won both that season and again in 1912, so without the split they might have won titles in each of their first 4 seasons.

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Western_Suburbs_Magpies&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Club honours")]

* **Premierships: 4** – 1930 beat St George; 1934 beat Easts; 1948 beat Balmain; 1952 beat South Sydney

* **NSWRL Third Grade**: 7 (1936, 1938, 1939, 1944, 1958, 1961, 1967)

* **[President's Cup](/wiki/Presidents_Cup_(Rugby_League) "Presidents Cup (Rugby League)")**: 5 (1925, 1945, 1947, 1958, 1992)

**Largest crowd**:

* [Lidcombe Oval](/wiki/Lidcombe_Oval "Lidcombe Oval"): 21,015 vs Parramatta (30 July 1978)

* [Pratten Park](/wiki/Pratten_Park "Pratten Park"): 12,407 vs St George (15 June 1964)

**Biggest win**: 62–5 vs Balmain (Lidcombe Oval, 31 March 1974)
